nativeRange.habitat: Coastal sand flats, sand slopes, and deep lagoons adjacent to reef structure, typically at depths of 3 to 35 metres. In the wild, the species is found hovering at the entrance of a shared pistol-shrimp burrow on substrates of fine sand or coral rubble.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5-8 cm deep for burrowing
loose coral rubble or shell fragments for the partner shrimp to build with
open sandy footprint near the front for visibility
low to moderate flow over the burrow zone
scattered live rock structure for cover

diet.notes: The Flagtail Shrimp Goby is carnivorous and generally accepts a varied meaty diet of frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, krill, and small carnivore pellets. Feeding at least twice daily in small portions is generally advised. Newly imported specimens have been reported to be shy at first and may need targeted feeding near the burrow entrance until they settle.

goodWith: pistol shrimp (Alpheus randalli — preferred symbiotic partner)
small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: conspecifics (territorial toward other Amblyeleotris yanoi unless a bonded pair)
other shrimp gobies competing for the same substrate territory
large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish, large hawkfish)
triggerfish and other crustacean-hunting species that would target the partner shrimp
very small ornamental shrimp (Sexy Shrimp, very small Periclimenes) that could be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 1977x1483. Sharp full lateral profile of a vibrant individual clearly showing the pale body, pinkish-orange vertical bars and the yellow-and-blue flag-shaped caudal fin - the key diagnostic features.
desertnaturalist (iNaturalist); 1943x1565. Clean in-situ lateral of a second individual on sand, showing the reddish-orange bars and yellow flag tail; slightly softer than the recommended pick.

Steve Childs; 1853x1482. Sharp shot of the goby standing sentry beside its Alpheus pistol-shrimp partner - depicts the defining symbiosis for this species. Head-on angle so the flag tail is not visible; generic file name so ID rests on the coloration and Commons categorisation.

summary: A medium-sized burrowing shrimp goby with a pale body, pinkish-orange bars and a striking yellow flag-shaped caudal fin. Forms a symbiotic burrow partnership with Randall's pistol shrimp on sandy reef slopes.
careNotes:
The Flagtail Shrimp Goby is one of the larger Amblyeleotris shrimp gobies kept in the hobby — males have been reported to reach around 13 cm, while females tend to stay considerably smaller at around 5 to 6 cm. This sexual size difference means a mated pair will look noticeably mismatched in the aquarium, with the male typically standing sentry at the burrow entrance while the smaller female stays closer to cover.

Like most members of its genus, this species naturally pairs with a pistol shrimp — most often Randall's Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus randalli), which excavates and maintains a shared burrow while the goby acts as a lookout. Tiger Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus bellulus) are reported as an acceptable alternative partner in captivity. To support this behaviour, a fine sand bed of at least 5 to 8 cm with a few flat rubble pieces for the shrimp to build under is generally advised.

A minimum aquarium volume of around 150 litres with a footprint of at least 90 cm is generally recommended for this species. The male can reach roughly 13 cm, and individuals tend to be territorial toward conspecifics, so a longer tank with a defined open sandy area gives the resident pair or single specimen room to establish a stable burrow territory.

A tightly fitting lid is important — like most gobies and dartfish, this species has been known to jump when startled. The Flagtail Shrimp Goby is generally considered reef-safe and ignores corals, but very small ornamental shrimp could be at risk, and conspecifics should be avoided unless deliberately introduced as a bonded pair.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Flagtail Shrimp Goby is poorly documented. Like related Amblyeleotris and Stonogobiops shrimp gobies, pairs are presumed to spawn inside the shared burrow with the male tending the eggs. Rearing the pelagic larvae would likely require very small live foods — rotifers initially, followed by copepod nauplii — and this larval-rearing stage is generally considered the main hurdle for hobbyist breeding attempts.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary hobby source for water parameters, minimum tank size (30 gal), reef safety, temperament, care level (Easy) and diet recommendations. The original manifest URL (the gobies collection root) did not surface this species directly; the specific product page was located via search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), wild distribution (Ryukyu Islands to Papua New Guinea), depth range (3-35 m), sexual size dimorphism (males to 13 cm SL, females to 5.8 cm SL), wild temperature range (26.9-29°C), and confirmation of Alpheus randalli as the symbiotic partner shrimp.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (100 L minimum, larger preferred), 13 cm max size, territorial/jumping behaviour, partner shrimp requirement, and reef-safe status. Cited as confirming source for the larger-male body size that drove the 150 L minimum decision.

additional.name: AquaInfo
additional.notes: Third hobby source confirming 100 L minimum (with 200 L preferred), 27-29°C temperature range, pH 8.1-8.4, carnivorous diet, symbiosis with Alpheus randalli, and pair/solitary social structure. Wikipedia (limited stub) was consulted only to confirm taxonomy and binomial authority.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning behaviour in captivity is poorly documented; like related shrimp gobies the male is presumed to tend a clutch inside the burrow, with pelagic larvae that need very small live first foods.
